lines.translation: Après l'entretien de Xuemei, l'entreprise l'a informée qu'elle commencerait à travailler la semaine prochaine. Xuemei et Ke Lin étaient très heureuses et ont décidé d'inviter des amis chez Xuemei pour fêter ça.

Le bon ami de Zhang Tianming, Li Zhe, est également venu en Chine. Il a accepté la suggestion de Tianming de faire un stage dans une entreprise multinationale. Du coup, la fête de célébration est aussi devenue une fête de bienvenue pour Li Zhe.
Zhang Tianming et Lisha retourneront bientôt aux États-Unis, ce rassemblement est donc aussi une fête d'adieu pour eux.
Outre Tianming, Lisha et Li Zhe, les invités comprenaient également Li Wen et Mark.
Ke Lin est allée au supermarché et a acheté beaucoup de boissons, de snacks et de fruits. Xuemei a préparé une fondue chinoise et a également commandé des plats à emporter dans un restaurant voisin.
Aujourd'hui, nous sommes tous réunis pour accueillir Li Zhe, pour dire au revoir à Tianming et Lisha, et aussi pour fêter que Xuemei a trouvé un emploi.
C'est ça. Tout le monde, ne vous gênez pas. Mangez bien, amusez-vous bien.
Xuemei, félicitations pour avoir trouvé un bon emploi. Quand commences-tu ?
Lundi prochain. Et vendredi, je dois partir en voyage d'affaires en Europe pour promouvoir des panneaux solaires et des batteries économes en énergie.

Des panneaux solaires et des batteries économes en énergie ? C'est génial ! Récemment, les produits écologiques liés à la protection de l'environnement et à la conservation de l'énergie se vendent très bien sur le marché.
Hé, Mark, Li Wen, venez par ici. Laissez-moi vous présenter. Voici Li Zhe, un ancien élève de notre université, originaire des États-Unis. Il vient d'arriver d'Amérique.
Bienvenue à Pékin.
Bonjour à tous ! J'espère pouvoir compter sur vous.
Li Zhe, dans quelle entreprise fais-tu ton stage à Pékin ?
J'ai déjà dit à Tianming. C'est l'entreprise où tu vas. Il ne t'a pas annoncé la nouvelle ? La personne qui t'a interviewée ce jour-là est le bon ami de mon frère aîné.
Vraiment ? Alors, ne sommes-nous pas collègues ? C'est génial. Nous pourrons nous entraider et nous soutenir mutuellement à l'avenir.
C'est pour ça que j'ai dit : « j'espère pouvoir compter sur vous ».
On ne sait pas encore qui aidera qui.
Mark, depuis combien de temps es-tu en Chine ?
Six ans.
Mark, quel genre de travail fais-tu en Chine ?
Je suis « freelance ». J'ai été professeur d'anglais, j'ai joué dans des séries télévisées et j'ai tourné des publicités. Parfois, je fais aussi de la traduction.
Je me demandais pourquoi tu me semblais un peu familier. J'ai vu la série télévisée dans laquelle tu as joué. Tu as très bien joué.
Je ne suis qu'un acteur de troisième ordre.
On dirait que tu as beaucoup d'opportunités d'emploi et que tu es très occupé.

Pas forcément. Parfois je suis occupé, d'autres fois je n'ai rien à faire. Pour être franc, le travail est un peu instable. Mais ça va, je m'en sors. Ici, je vis très heureux et je me suis fait beaucoup d'amis chinois.
Pas étonnant que ton chinois soit si bon.
Li Zhe, l'ami de ton frère est vraiment quelqu'un. Il est devenu directeur général si jeune.
Non seulement il s'y connaît en ventes, mais il est aussi un très bon manager. J'entends dire que sous sa direction, l'entreprise va de mieux en mieux.
Il y a de plus en plus d'étrangers en Chine maintenant. T'es-tu habitué à la vie ici ?
Qu'est-ce que tu en penses ? Chez toi, je pratique le tai-chi avec tes parents tous les jours et je mange la nourriture délicieuse et saine qu'ils préparent. Je n'ai même plus envie de rentrer.
Moi, j'ai encore moins de problèmes.
Tu écris toujours ton blog ?
Bien sûr. Ici, je vois et j'entends de nouvelles choses tous les jours. Je n'aurai jamais de panne d'inspiration.
Pas besoin de le dire. Sinon, comment aurais-je pu vivre ici si longtemps ? J'ai l'impression de m'être déjà intégré à cette société.
Au début, j'avais peur de ne pas pouvoir m'adapter à la vie ici. Après vous avoir tous entendus, je suis beaucoup plus soulagé.

Maintenant, les étrangers viennent en Chine pour faire carrière, et les Chinois partent à l'étranger pour étudier et travailler. Les contacts de chacun se sont multipliés, les relations se sont resserrées, et le monde est devenu plus petit.
Li Wen l'a si bien dit. Les amis, venez, levons nos verres. Portons un toast à notre amitié !
À vos carrières réussies en Chine !
Bon voyage à Tianming et Lisha !
À la santé de tous !
Santé !

language_notes.label: a
language_notes.explanation_en: 接风/接风 désigne l'action d'accueillir et de divertir un ami qui vient de rentrer d'un long voyage « venteux ». Cette expression est souvent utilisée avec 洗尘/洗尘 [zh:xǐchén], qui signifie littéralement « laver la poussière avec du vin ». Par exemple, on dit 给(为)李哲接风洗尘/给(为)李哲接风洗尘 pour organiser un banquet en l'honneur de Li Zhe et le débarrasser de la poussière de son long voyage.

language_notes.label: b
language_notes.explanation_en: 饯行/饯行 signifie littéralement accompagner un ami pour un long voyage avec de la nourriture conservée dans du sucre ou du miel. Les fruits confits s'appellent 蜜饯/蜜饯 [zh:mìjiàn], où 蜜 signifie miel. Comme 接风洗尘/接风洗尘, 饯行/饯行 est une expression littéraire. Les prépositions 给/给 ou 为/为 peuvent également être utilisées, par exemple dans 给(为)天明和丽莎饯行/给(为)天明和丽莎饯行.

language_notes.label: c
language_notes.explanation_en: 叫外卖/叫外卖, qui signifie littéralement « crier à emporter », veut dire « commander des plats à emporter ». On peut aussi dire 叫车/叫车 pour « héler un taxi » et 叫菜/叫菜 pour « commander un plat ».

language_notes.label: d
language_notes.explanation_en: 火 peut décrire une affaire florissante et dynamique ou un produit qui se vend « comme des petits pains ».

language_notes.label: e
language_notes.explanation_en: 请多关照/请多关照 est une expression figée qui signifie approximativement « S'il vous plaît, soyez généreux avec votre aide et votre considération ». Les gens l'utilisent souvent lorsqu'ils se présentent pour la première fois à de nouveaux collègues. L'expression vise à exprimer la modestie.

language_notes.label: f
language_notes.explanation_en: 自由职业者/自由职业者 désigne les membres des « professions libérales », 自由职业/自由职业, c'est-à-dire des métiers qui mettent l'accent sur des qualifications professionnelles spéciales et la responsabilité individuelle, comme la médecine et le droit. Le terme a été introduit de l'Occident. Ici, Mark fait une blague : il met l'accent sur 自由 et veut dire qu'il est un travailleur indépendant.

language_notes.label: g
language_notes.explanation_en: 说白了/说白了 signifie « parler franchement ». 白 ici veut dire « simple » ou « sans fioritures ».

grammar_sections.title: Résumé de l'ordre des mots en chinois
grammar_sections.explanation_en: La structure de base de la phrase chinoise est : Sujet/agent, puis un adverbe, le verbe, un complément et enfin l'objet ou le destinataire de l'action. Les attributs en chinois apparaissent avant ce qu'ils modifient. Cependant, l'ordre des mots en chinois peut varier dans des types de phrases spécifiques. Par exemple, dans les phrases à thème-commentaire, l'information connue peut apparaître au début de la phrase comme thème. Les phrases avec 把/把 [zh:bǎ] et 被/被 [zh:bèi] s'écartent également de l'ordre régulier des phrases. D'autres principes qui affectent l'ordre des mots incluent l'arrangement des éléments du général au spécifique, comme dans les dates et les adresses, et l'arrangement des événements ou des situations de manière chronologique.

patterns.meaning_en: Structure de phrase à thème-commentaire, où le thème est une information connue.

patterns.pattern: Subject + 把 + Direct Object + Verb + Other Elements
Recipient of the Action + 被 + Agent + Verb + Complement

usage_notes: Du général au spécifique : Les adresses et les dates en chinois sont écrites de l'unité la plus grande à la plus petite, par exemple, Pays, Ville, District, ce qui est l'exact opposé de l'organisation en français.

Organisation des événements ou situations selon leur ordre chronologique : Les événements ou situations décrits dans une phrase chinoise sont généralement dans l'ordre chronologique, c'est-à-dire que ce qui se passe en premier est énoncé en premier. Par exemple, dans '[zh:我常常在图书馆学习]' (J'étudie souvent à la bibliothèque), les événements sont énoncés dans leur ordre d'apparition : être à la bibliothèque, puis étudier.

Dans '[zh:表哥明天会开车到机场来接我们]' (Mon cousin viendra nous chercher à l'aéroport en voiture demain), les événements sont énoncés dans leur ordre d'apparition : conduire, arriver à l'aéroport, puis nous chercher.

Dans '[zh:我在房间用电脑写文章]' (J'écrivais un article sur mon ordinateur dans ma chambre), les événements sont énoncés dans leur ordre d'apparition : être dans la chambre, utiliser l'ordinateur, puis écrire l'article.

grammar_sections.title: Révision de la construction [zh:把] (II)
grammar_sections.explanation_en:
La construction [zh:把] doit être utilisée si l'objet dans une phrase est spécifique ou connu à la fois de l'interlocuteur et de l'auditeur, si l'agent de l'action a également été explicitement mentionné, et si les circonstances suivantes sont réunies :
1. Il y a un objet indirect dans la phrase.
2. Il y a une expression indiquant l'emplacement de l'objet après l'exécution de l'action.
3. Le verbe est suivi du complément [zh:成].
Les phrases avec la construction [zh:把] nécessitent un sujet, c'est-à-dire un agent responsable de l'action. Dans certaines circonstances, le sujet peut être omis s'il est clairement compris. Si l'agent responsable de l'action est inconnu, non spécifié ou n'est pas le centre d'intérêt, la construction [zh:把] n'a pas besoin d'être utilisée.

patterns.pattern: Subject + 把 + Object + Verb + Other Elements (e.g., Indirect Object, Location, Complement)
patterns.pattern_pinyin: Subject + bǎ + Object + Verb + Other Elements
patterns.meaning_en: Cette structure est utilisée pour montrer ce qui arrive à l'objet, en soulignant le résultat ou l'influence de l'action sur celui-ci.

usage_notes: Dans les phrases avec [zh:把], les adverbes négatifs doivent être placés avant [zh:把].
Les compléments de potentiel ne peuvent pas être utilisés dans les phrases avec [zh:把].

La construction avec 把 est l'une des structures les plus complexes de la grammaire chinoise. L'utilisation de la construction avec 把 dépend aussi du contexte linguistique général et d'autres facteurs. En vous exerçant à reconnaître et à utiliser la construction avec 把, vous en saisirez vite les nuances.

common_errors.correct: 你把这张照片送给我吧。
common_errors.explanation_en: Quand une phrase a un objet indirect, la construction avec 把 est souvent nécessaire pour indiquer l'action effectuée sur l'objet direct.

common_errors.wrong: 他放了筷子和方便面在桌子上。
common_errors.correct: 他把筷子和方便面放在了桌子上。
common_errors.explanation_en: Quand une phrase indique l'emplacement résultant d'un objet après une action, la construction avec 把 est nécessaire.

common_errors.wrong: 他变宿舍房间成了一个垃圾场。
common_errors.correct: 他把宿舍房间变成了一个垃圾场。
common_errors.explanation_en: Quand le verbe est suivi du complément de résultat 成, la construction avec 把 est nécessaire.

common_errors.wrong: 我把你要的地图没带来。
common_errors.correct: 我没把你要的地图带来。
common_errors.explanation_en: Dans les phrases avec 把, les adverbes négatifs comme 没 doivent être placés avant 把.

common_errors.wrong: 要是把护照不放在我这里……
common_errors.correct: 要是不把护照放在我这里……
common_errors.explanation_en: Dans les phrases avec 把, les adverbes négatifs comme 不 doivent être placés avant 把.

common_errors.wrong: 我把购房合同找不到了。
common_errors.explanation_en: Les compléments de potentiel, comme 找得到/找不到, ne peuvent pas être utilisés dans les phrases avec 把. Une structure de phrase différente doit être utilisée, par exemple, 我找不到了购房合同。

common_errors.wrong: 舅舅说舅妈把碗洗不干净。
common_errors.explanation_en: Les compléments de potentiel, comme 洗得干净/洗不干净, ne peuvent pas être utilisés dans les phrases avec 把. Une structure de phrase différente doit être utilisée, par exemple, 舅舅说舅妈洗不干净碗。

hanzi: 雪梅面试后,那家公司通知她下周开始上班。雪梅和柯林都很高兴,他们决定请朋友们到雪梅住的地方来聚会,庆祝一下。
张天明的好朋友李哲也来到中国了,他接受了天明的建议,到一家跨国公司来实习。结果,庆祝晚会也成了给李哲接风的晚会。
张天明和丽莎很快就要回美国了,所以这次聚会也是给他们饯行的晚会。
来的客人除了天明、丽莎、李哲外,还有李文和马克。
柯林去超市买了很多饮料、零食和水果。雪梅准备了一个火锅,还从附近的一家餐馆叫了外卖。

Zhāng Tiānmíng de hǎo péngyou Lǐ Zhé yě láidào Zhōngguó le tā jiēshòu le Tiānmíng de jiànyì dào yì jiā kuàguó gōngsī lái shíxí jiéguǒ qìngzhù wǎnhuì yě chéng le gěi Lǐ Zhé jiēfēng de wǎnhuì
Zhāng Tiānmíng hé Lìshā hěn kuài jiù yào huí Měiguó le suǒyǐ zhè cì jùhuì yě shì gěi tāmen jiànxíng de wǎnhuì

Kē Lín qù chāoshì mǎi le hěn duō yǐnliào língshí hé shuǐguǒ Xuěméi zhǔnbèi le yí ge huǒguō hái cóng fùjìn de yì jiā cānguǎn jiào le wàimài
translation: After Xuemei's interview, the company notified her to start work next week. Xuemei and Ke Lin were very happy, and they decided to invite friends over to Xuemei's place for a party to celebrate.

Zhang Tianming's good friend Li Zhe has also come to China. He accepted Tianming's suggestion to intern at a multinational company. As a result, the celebratory party also became a welcome party for Li Zhe.
Zhang Tianming and Lisha will be returning to the US soon, so this gathering is also a farewell party for them.
Besides Tianming, Lisha, and Li Zhe, the guests who came also included Li Wen and Mark.
Ke Lin went to the supermarket and bought a lot of drinks, snacks, and fruit. Xuemei prepared a hot pot and also ordered takeout from a nearby restaurant.

global_config.teaching_style: A clear, encouraging, and structured approach, breaking down sentences and vocabulary for easy comprehension.
global_config.visual_style: Clean, modern educational cards with animated text and highlights.

segments.segment_title: 雪梅 gets a job offer
segments.teaching_goal: To understand how to talk about job interviews and starting a new job.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's start with the first part of the sentence. It says: After Xuemei's interview, the company notified her to start work next week. The phrase [zh:面试后] means 'after the interview.' [zh:面试] is 'interview' and [zh:后] means 'after.' The company, [zh:公司], then [zh:通知] or 'notified' her to [zh:开始上班], 'start work,' next week, which is [zh:下周]. This sentence sets the happy tone for our story.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 雪梅面试后,那家公司通知她下周开始上班。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Xuěméi miànshì hòu, nà jiā gōngsī tōngzhī tā xià zhōu kāishǐ shàngbān.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: After Xuemei's interview, the company notified her to start work next week.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A noun for 'week'. '下周' means 'next week'.

segments.visual_notes: Animate the text sequentially. Highlight '面试后', '通知', and '下周开始上班' as they are mentioned.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to express happiness and suggest a party.

narration.subtitle_en: The story continues. Xuemei and Ke Lin were very happy. The key phrase here is [zh:很高兴]. They decided to invite friends for a party to celebrate. [zh:决定] means 'to decide.' And what did they decide? To have a [zh:聚会], which means 'get-together' or 'party.' The purpose is to [zh:庆祝一下], 'to celebrate a bit.' This shows their excitement about the new job.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 雪梅和柯林都很高兴,他们决定请朋友们到雪梅住的地方来聚会,庆祝一下。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Xuěméi hé Kē Lín dōu hěn gāoxìng, tāmen juédìng qǐng péngyoumen dào Xuěméi zhù de dìfang lái jùhuì, qìngzhù yíxià.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Xuemei and Ke Lin were very happy, and they decided to invite friends over to Xuemei's place for a party to celebrate.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Can be a verb 'to get together' or a noun 'a party'. Here it's used as a verb in the phrase '来聚会' (come to get together).
A verb used for celebrating happy occasions like birthdays, holidays, or achievements.

segments.visual_notes: Highlight '很高兴', '决定', '聚会', and '庆祝一下' in sequence.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand how to talk about accepting suggestions and starting an internship.

narration.subtitle_en: Now, a new character is introduced. Zhang Tianming's good friend, Li Zhe, has also come to China. The sentence says he [zh:接受] or 'accepted' Tianming's [zh:建议], 'suggestion.' And what was the suggestion? To come to a multinational company, a [zh:跨国公司], for an internship, or [zh:实习]. This sets up another reason for the party.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 张天明的好朋友李哲也来到中国了,他接受了天明的建议,到一家跨国公司来实习。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Zhāng Tiānmíng de hǎo péngyou Lǐ Zhé yě láidào Zhōngguó le, tā jiēshòu le Tiānmíng de jiànyì, dào yī jiā kuàguó gōngsī lái shíxí.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Zhang Tianming's good friend Li Zhe has also come to China. He accepted Tianming's suggestion to intern at a multinational company.

highlight_words.explanation_en: This verb means to accept something, like a suggestion, an invitation, or a gift.

segments.visual_notes: Highlight '接受', '建议', and '实习' as they are explained.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the term for a welcome party.

narration.subtitle_en: This sentence explains the outcome. [zh:结果] means 'as a result.' The celebratory party, [zh:庆祝晚会], also 'became', [zh:成了], a welcome party for Li Zhe. The special term used here is [zh:接风]. A [zh:接风的晚会] is a party to welcome someone who has traveled from afar. It's a very common cultural practice in China.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 结果,庆祝晚会也成了给李哲接风的晚会。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Jiéguǒ, qìngzhù wǎnhuì yě chéng le gěi Lǐ Zhé jiēfēng de wǎnhuì.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: As a result, the celebratory party also became a welcome party for Li Zhe.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A verb referring to the act of welcoming a visitor from afar with a meal. It literally means 'to catch the wind'.

segments.visual_notes: Animate the text to show '庆祝晚会' transforming into '接风的晚会'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the term for a farewell party.

narration.subtitle_en: The party has yet another purpose! Zhang Tianming and Lisha are returning to the US soon. The phrase [zh:很快就要...了] indicates something will happen soon. Because of this, the party is also a farewell party for them. The specific word for this is [zh:饯行]. So, a [zh:饯行的晚会] is a farewell party. Notice how this one party serves three different functions!

on_screen_text.focus_text: 张天明和丽莎很快就要回美国了,所以这次聚会也是给他们饯行的晚会。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Zhāng Tiānmíng hé Lìshā hěn kuài jiù yào huí Měiguó le, suǒyǐ zhè cì jùhuì yě shì gěi tāmen jiànxíng de wǎnhuì.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Zhang Tianming and Lisha will be returning to the US soon, so this gathering is also a farewell party for them.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A verb for hosting a farewell meal for someone who is about to go on a long journey.

segments.visual_notes: Highlight '很快就要...了' and '饯行'. Maybe show an icon for 'welcome' and 'farewell' next to each other.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to list people using '除了...外, 还...'.

narration.subtitle_en: This sentence lists the guests at the party. The word for guest is [zh:客人]. It uses a very useful structure: [zh:除了...外, 还有...], which means 'besides... also...'. So, besides Tianming, Lisha, and Li Zhe, the guests who came also included Li Wen and Mark. This structure is great for listing items or people.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 来的客人除了天明、丽莎、李哲外,还有李文和马克。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Lái de kèrén chúle Tiānmíng, Lìshā, Lǐ Zhé wài, hái yǒu Lǐ Wén hé Mǎkè.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Besides Tianming, Lisha, and Li Zhe, the guests who came also included Li Wen and Mark.

highlight_words.explanation_en: The person who is invited to a home or an event. '客' means guest and '人' means person.

segments.visual_notes: Visually group the first set of names with '除了...外' and the second set with '还有'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn vocabulary for party supplies like drinks, snacks, and fruit.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's see how they prepared for the party. Ke Lin went to the supermarket, [zh:超市], and bought many things. He bought [zh:饮料] (drinks), [zh:零食] (snacks), and [zh:水果] (fruit). These are all essential words for shopping or talking about food. Notice the use of [zh:很多], meaning 'a lot of'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 柯林去超市买了很多饮料、零食和水果。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Kē Lín qù chāoshì mǎi le hěn duō yǐnliào, língshí hé shuǐguǒ.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Ke Lin went to the supermarket and bought a lot of drinks, snacks, and fruit.

segments.visual_notes: Show icons for drinks, snacks, and fruit as the words are mentioned.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn about hotpot and how to say 'order takeout'.

narration.subtitle_en: While Ke Lin was shopping, Xuemei was also busy. She [zh:准备了], or 'prepared,' a [zh:火锅], which is a Chinese hotpot. It's a very popular communal meal for parties. In addition, she also ordered takeout from a nearby restaurant. The phrase for this is [zh:叫了外卖]. This is an extremely common and useful phrase in modern China.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 雪梅准备了一个火锅,还从附近的一家餐馆叫了外卖。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Xuěméi zhǔnbèi le yí ge huǒguō, hái cóng fùjìn de yì jiā cānguǎn jiào le wàimài.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Xuemei prepared a hot pot and also ordered takeout from a nearby restaurant.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A popular Chinese dish where a simmering pot of soup stock is placed at the center of the table, and various ingredients are cooked in it. '火' means fire and '锅' means pot.

segments.visual_notes: Show an image of a hotpot, then an animation of a phone ordering food for '叫外卖'.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand the versatile usage of the verb '叫' for ordering or hailing services.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's look closer at the phrase [zh:叫外卖]. The verb [zh:叫] literally means 'to shout' or 'to call'. But here, it's used to mean 'to order' or 'to hail'. You can use it in other contexts too. For example, you can [zh:叫车] to hail a cab, or [zh:叫菜] to order dishes at a restaurant. It's a very practical verb for daily life.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 叫外卖 / 叫车 / 叫菜
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: jiào wàimài / jiào chē / jiào cài
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: to order takeout / to hail a cab / to order a dish

highlight_words.explanation_en: A common phrase where '叫' means 'to order' and '外卖' means 'takeout'.

segments.visual_notes: Display the three phrases with corresponding icons: a food delivery box, a taxi, and a menu.

segments.segment_title: The Toast: Three Reasons to Celebrate
segments.teaching_goal: To understand a formal toast that summarizes the reasons for a gathering.

narration.subtitle_en: Here, one of the friends is making a toast, explaining why they've gathered. [zh:今天我们大家聚在一起] means 'Today we are all gathered together.' The structure [zh:是为了] introduces the purpose. What are the reasons? First, [zh:给李哲接风], to welcome Li Zhe. Second, [zh:给天明和丽莎饯行], to send off Tianming and Lisha. And finally, [zh:也庆祝雪梅找到工作], to also celebrate Xuemei finding a job. This sentence neatly summarizes the entire situation.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 今天我们大家聚在一起,是为了给李哲接风,给天明和丽莎饯行,也庆祝雪梅找到工作。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Jīntiān wǒmen dàjiā jù zài yìqǐ, shì wèile gěi Lǐ Zhé jiēfēng, gěi Tiānmíng hé Lìshā jiànxíng, yě qìngzhù Xuěméi zhǎodào gōngzuò.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Today we're all gathered together to welcome Li Zhe, to see Tianming and Lisha off, and also to celebrate Xuemei finding a job.

highlight_words.explanation_en: The verb 'to gather' or 'to get together'. It's the root of the word '聚会' (party).

segments.visual_notes: Display the three reasons as a list on screen, highlighting each one as it's mentioned.

segments.segment_title: Welcome vs. Farewell: 接风 & 饯行
segments.teaching_goal: To understand the cultural significance and usage of '接风' and '饯行'.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's focus on two important cultural terms: [zh:接风] and [zh:饯行]. They are opposites. [zh:接风] is a meal to welcome someone who has just arrived from a long trip. It literally means 'to meet the wind'. [zh:饯行] is a farewell meal for someone who is about to leave on a long journey. Both are literary expressions that show hospitality and care for friends. You often use them with the preposition [zh:给], as in [zh:给朋友接风] or [zh:给朋友饯行].

on_screen_text.focus_text: 接风 / 饯行
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: jiēfēng / jiànxíng
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: to host a welcome meal / to host a farewell meal
on_screen_text.notes: 接风 (jiēfēng): For arrivals. 饯行 (jiànxíng): For departures.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Refers to welcoming and entertaining a friend who has just returned from a long, 'windy' trip.
Refers to sending off a friend on a long trip with a meal. A literary expression.

segments.visual_notes: Show two columns. One for '接风' with an 'arrival' icon, and one for '饯行' with a 'departure' icon.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn common hosting phrases like 'don't be shy' and 'eat well'.

narration.subtitle_en: This is a classic line from a host. [zh:对] means 'That's right.' Then, [zh:大家不要客气] tells everyone 'don't be polite' or 'make yourselves at home.' Finally, the host encourages guests to [zh:好好吃, 好好玩儿] which means 'eat well and have fun.' The reduplication of [zh:好好] emphasizes doing the action well and with enjoyment. It's a very warm and common expression.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 对,大家不要客气,好好吃,好好玩儿。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Duì, dàjiā búyào kèqi, hǎohāo chī, hǎohāo wánr.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: That's right. Everyone, don't be shy. Eat well, have fun.

segments.visual_notes: Highlight '不要客气' and the '好好' structure.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to congratulate someone and ask when they start work.

narration.subtitle_en: Here, a friend congratulates Xuemei. The word for congratulations is [zh:恭喜]. So, [zh:恭喜你找到了好工作] means 'Congratulations on finding a good job.' Then, they ask a practical question: [zh:什么时候上班?], which means 'When do you start work?'. [zh:什么时候] is the standard way to ask 'when'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 雪梅,恭喜你找到了好工作。什么时候上班?
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Xuěméi, gōngxǐ nǐ zhǎodào le hǎo gōngzuò. Shénme shíhou shàngbān?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Xuemei, congratulations on finding a good job. When do you start?

segments.visual_notes: Show a 'congratulations' graphic for '恭喜'. Highlight the question '什么时候上班?'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn vocabulary related to business trips and products.

narration.subtitle_en: Xuemei describes her busy schedule. She starts next Monday, but on Friday she already has to go to Europe on a business trip, or [zh:出差]. What for? To [zh:推销], or 'promote the sale of,' two products: [zh:太阳能板] (solar panels) and [zh:节能电池] (energy-saving batteries). This shows her new job is in the green energy sector.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 下个星期一,星期五就要到欧洲出差,推销太阳能板和节能电池。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Xià ge xīngqīyī, xīngqīwǔ jiù yào dào Ōuzhōu chū chāi, tuīxiāo tàiyángnéng bǎn hé jiénéng diànchí.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Next Monday. And on Friday I have to go to Europe on a business trip to promote solar panels and energy-saving batteries.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A verb-object phrase meaning to be away on business.
A verb meaning to market or promote a product.
'电' means electricity and '池' means pool or pond, so it's a 'pool of electricity'.

segments.visual_notes: Show icons for a suitcase (出差), a graph (推销), a solar panel, and a battery.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to express strong approval.

narration.subtitle_en: A friend reacts positively to Xuemei's new job. They exclaim, 'Solar panels and energy-saving batteries? That's great!' The phrase [zh:太好了] is a very common way to express strong approval or excitement. It literally means 'too good,' but it's used to mean 'excellent' or 'wonderful'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 太阳能板和节能电池?太好了!
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Tàiyángnéng bǎn hé jiénéng diànchí? Tài hǎo le!
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Solar panels and energy-saving batteries? That's great!

segments.visual_notes: Animate '太好了!' with sparkles or a thumbs-up icon.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand how to talk about market trends and the special meaning of '火'.

narration.subtitle_en: The friend elaborates on why it's a good field. 'Recently, green products related to environmental protection and energy conservation have been selling very well.' Key terms are [zh:环保] (environmental protection) and [zh:节能] (energy conservation). The most interesting word is at the end: [zh:火]. While it means 'fire,' here it's slang for 'popular' or 'selling like hotcakes.' So, [zh:卖得很火] means 'selling very well'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 最近跟环保和节能有关系的绿色产品市场上都卖得很火。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Zuìjìn gēn huánbǎo hé jiénéng yǒu guānxì de lǜsè chǎnpǐn shìchǎng shàng dōu mài de hěn huǒ.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Recently, green products related to environmental protection and energy conservation have been selling very well on the market.

segments.visual_notes: Highlight '环保', '节能', and especially '火' with a fire animation.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand the slang usage of '火' to mean 'popular' or 'booming'.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's focus on the word [zh:火]. Its basic meaning is 'fire'. However, in modern slang, it's often used to describe something that is extremely popular, booming, or 'hot'. For example, if a song is very popular, you can say '这首歌很火'. If a restaurant's business is flourishing, you can say '这家餐馆的生意很火'. In our text, [zh:卖得很火] means the products are selling extremely well, just like a spreading fire.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 火
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: huǒ
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: fire; (slang) popular, booming, 'hot'
on_screen_text.notes: Example: 这个产品很火 (This product is very popular).

highlight_words.explanation_en: Can describe a brisk, flourishing business or a product that is 'selling like hotcakes'.

segments.visual_notes: Show the character '火' and an animation of a fire. Then, show examples like a trending song or a busy restaurant with the '火' character next to them.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to get someone's attention and initiate an introduction.

narration.subtitle_en: The scene opens with someone calling their friends over. The word [zh:哎] is an interjection, similar to 'Hey!' used to get someone's attention. [zh:你们过来] means 'you all come over.' Then, the speaker says [zh:我来介绍一下], which is a very common and polite way to say 'Let me introduce...' The phrase [zh:一下] softens the action, making it sound more casual, like 'introduce real quick'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 哎,马克,李文,你们过来,我来介绍一下,
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Āi, Mǎkè, Lǐ Wén, nǐmen guòlái, wǒ lái jièshào yíxià,
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Hey, Mark, Li Wen, come over here. Let me introduce you.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A directional complement indicating movement towards the speaker.
A key verb for introducing people or things.

segments.visual_notes: Animate an arrow pointing towards the speaker for '过来'. Highlight '介绍一下' as a set phrase.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to introduce someone's identity and origin, specifically using '校友' and '刚'.

narration.subtitle_en: Continuing the introduction, the speaker says [zh:这是我们美国大学的校友]. The word [zh:校友] means 'alumnus' or 'schoolmate'. It's formed by [zh:校], meaning school, and [zh:友], meaning friend. Then, they introduce his name, Li Zhe, and add that he [zh:刚从美国来]. The adverb [zh:刚] means 'just' or 'recently', indicating the action happened a short time ago. So, he just came from America.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 这是我们美国大学的校友,李哲,刚从美国来。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: zhè shì wǒmen Měiguó dàxué de xiàoyǒu, Lǐ Zhé, gāng cóng Měiguó lái.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: This is our university alumnus from the US, Li Zhe. He just came from America.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A person who attended the same school.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: Composed of 校 (xiào, school) and 友 (yǒu, friend), literally a 'school friend'.

highlight_words.explanation_en: An adverb indicating that an action took place in the recent past.

segments.visual_notes: Show a university icon next to '校友'. Use a clock icon rewinding slightly for '刚'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the standard phrase for welcoming someone to a place.

narration.subtitle_en: This is a simple and very common welcoming phrase. [zh:欢迎] means 'welcome'. You can use it before a person's name or, as in this case, before a place. [zh:欢迎来北京] literally means 'Welcome to come to Beijing'. It's the standard way to say 'Welcome to Beijing'. You can replace Beijing with any other city or place, like [zh:欢迎来上海] or [zh:欢迎来我家].

on_screen_text.focus_text: 欢迎来北京。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Huānyíng lái Běijīng.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Welcome to Beijing.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Used to greet guests or visitors.

segments.visual_notes: Show an animation of a welcoming gesture or an icon of Beijing's landmarks.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to greet a group and use a key polite expression when meeting people for the first time.

narration.subtitle_en: Li Zhe responds with two important phrases. First, [zh:大家好], which means 'Hello, everyone'. It's the plural form of 'hello'. Then he says [zh:请多关照]. This is a very common and polite set phrase used when you meet people for the first time in a new environment, like a new job or school. It's a way of showing modesty and asking for their future help and guidance. We'll look at this phrase more closely.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 大家好!请多关照。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Dàjiā hǎo! Qǐng duō guānzhào.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Hello, everyone! Please look after me.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A greeting used when addressing a group of people.
A polite, formulaic expression used when meeting new people, showing modesty.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a speech bubble from Li Zhe to the group. Put a star or highlight on '请多关照'.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand the cultural meaning and appropriate context for using '请多关照'.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's focus on [zh:请多关照]. As noted in the textbook, this is a formulaic expression that conveys modesty. It literally translates to 'please give much care and consideration'. You say this when introducing yourself to new colleagues or group members for the first time. It's a way of being humble and establishing a friendly, cooperative relationship from the start. It implies you're new and might need their help in the future.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 请多关照
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: qǐng duō guānzhào
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Please be liberal with your help and consideration.
on_screen_text.notes: A polite phrase for first meetings in a new group.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A polite word used to make a request.
Indicates a large amount or high degree.

highlight_words.translation: to look after, to care for
highlight_words.explanation_en: To give consideration and help to someone.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to ask someone which company they are interning at.

narration.subtitle_en: Here, a question is asked to Li Zhe. It starts with his name to get his attention. The core of the question is [zh:你来北京哪家公司实习]. Let's break it down. [zh:哪家] means 'which', and it's used with the measure word [zh:家] for companies. So, [zh:哪家公司] means 'which company'. The verb at the end is [zh:实习], which means 'to intern' or 'to do an internship'. The sentence structure is Subject-Place-Which Company-Verb.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 李哲,你来北京哪家公司实习?
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Lǐ Zhé, nǐ lái Běijīng nǎ jiā gōngsī shíxí?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Li Zhe, which company are you interning at in Beijing?

highlight_words.explanation_en: A business or firm.
To work temporarily in a professional setting to gain experience.

segments.visual_notes: Highlight '哪家公司' as a question phrase. Show an office building icon for '公司'.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand how to express 'already' and identify a specific item.

narration.subtitle_en: Li Zhe reveals a surprise. He starts with [zh:我已经告诉天明了]. The structure [zh:已经...了] means 'already'. So, 'I already told Tianming'. Then he clarifies which company: [zh:就是你要去的那个公司]. The phrase [zh:就是] adds emphasis, like 'it's precisely...' or 'it's exactly...'. The part [zh:你要去的那个公司] is a descriptive clause meaning 'that company that you are going to'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我已经告诉天明了,就是你要去的那个公司。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Wǒ yǐjīng gàosu Tiānmíng le, jiùshì nǐ yào qù de nàge gōngsī.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I already told Tianming. It's the company you're going to.

highlight_words.explanation_en: An adverb indicating that an action has been completed.
To inform someone of something.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a checkmark next to '已经...了'. Draw a circle connecting '公司' back to the previous question.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand a question using the '把' construction and the '是...的' structure for emphasis.

narration.subtitle_en: The surprises continue. First, a question: [zh:他没把这个消息告诉你]? This uses the [zh:把] construction in a negative question. [zh:消息] means 'news' or 'message'. The question means 'He didn't tell you this news?'. Then, another revelation: [zh:那天面试你的是我哥哥的好朋友]. This uses the [zh:是...的] structure to emphasize the person who did the action. The person who interviewed you, [zh:面试你的], was my older brother's good friend.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 他没把这个消息告诉你?那天面试你的是我哥哥的好朋友。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Tā méi bǎ zhège xiāoxi gàosu nǐ? Nàtiān miànshì nǐ de shì wǒ gēge de hǎo péngyou.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: He didn't tell you this news? The person who interviewed you that day is my older brother's good friend.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Information about a recent event.
A formal meeting for a job application.

segments.visual_notes: Highlight the '没把...告诉' structure. For the second sentence, highlight '面试你的是' and connect it to '好朋友'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to express surprise and ask a rhetorical question about a new status.

narration.subtitle_en: Reacting to the news, the speaker first says [zh:真的?], meaning 'Really?'. Then, they ask a rhetorical question: [zh:那我们不就是同事了吗]? The structure [zh:不就是...了吗] isn't a real question; it's like saying 'Then doesn't that mean we're colleagues?' or 'So we're colleagues, then!'. The word [zh:同事] means 'colleague' or 'coworker', combining [zh:同] (same) and [zh:事] (work, affair).

on_screen_text.focus_text: 真的?那我们不就是同事了吗?
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Zhēn de? Nà wǒmen bú jiùshì tóngshì le ma?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Really? Then aren't we colleagues?

highlight_words.explanation_en: Used to express surprise or seek confirmation.
A person with whom one works.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: Composed of 同 (tóng, same) and 事 (shì, work/affair), literally 'same work'.

segments.visual_notes: Use an exclamation mark or surprised emoji for '真的?'. Highlight '不就是...了吗' as a rhetorical pattern.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to express future cooperation using '互相'.

narration.subtitle_en: Expressing happiness, the speaker says [zh:太好了], 'That's great!'. Then they talk about the future: [zh:以后我们可以互相帮助,互相照顾]. [zh:以后] means 'in the future'. The key word here is [zh:互相], which means 'mutually' or 'each other'. It indicates a reciprocal action. So, [zh:互相帮助] is 'to help each other', and [zh:互相照顾] is 'to look after each other'. This shows a positive attitude towards their future collaboration.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 太好了,以后我们可以互相帮助,互相照顾。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Tài hǎo le, yǐhòu wǒmen kěyǐ hùxiāng bāngzhù, hùxiāng zhàogù.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: That's great. We can help and look after each other in the future.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Refers to a time that is to come.
Indicates that an action is reciprocal between two or more parties.

segments.visual_notes: Show two figures with arrows pointing back and forth between them to illustrate '互相'.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand how '所以' and '嘛' are used to connect ideas and add tone.

narration.subtitle_en: Li Zhe cleverly links back to his first statement. He says, [zh:所以我说请多关照嘛]. [zh:所以] means 'so' or 'that's why'. He's explaining the reason he said [zh:请多关照] earlier. The final particle [zh:嘛] is important here. It adds a tone of 'of course' or 'as you can see', implying that the reason should now be obvious to everyone. It reinforces his point in a lighthearted way.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 所以我说请多关照嘛。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Suǒyǐ wǒ shuō qǐng duō guānzhào ma.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: That's why I said, 'please look after me'.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A conjunction used to indicate a result or conclusion.
A sentence-final particle indicating that something is obvious.

segments.visual_notes: Draw an arrow from this sentence back to the earlier '请多关照' to show the connection.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn a polite and humble way to respond to '请多关照'.

narration.subtitle_en: This is a classic, humble response. [zh:还不知道谁关照谁呢]. Let's break it down. [zh:还不知道...呢] means 'it's still not known yet...' or 'we don't know yet...'. The core is [zh:谁关照谁], 'who will look after whom'. By saying this, the speaker is humbly suggesting that Li Zhe might be the one helping them, not the other way around. It's a friendly and modest way to deflect the formality of [zh:请多关照].

on_screen_text.focus_text: 还不知道谁关照谁呢。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Hái bù zhīdào shéi guānzhào shéi ne.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: We don't know who will be looking after whom yet.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Indicates a situation is still ongoing or unchanged.
An interrogative pronoun used to ask about a person.

segments.visual_notes: Show two people with a question mark between them to illustrate '谁关照谁'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the standard question pattern for asking about the duration of time.

narration.subtitle_en: The conversation shifts to Mark. The question is [zh:你来中国多长时间了]? This is a very useful pattern for asking 'how long' someone has been doing something or been somewhere. [zh:多长时间] literally means 'how long a period of time'. The structure is Subject + Verb/Action + [zh:多长时间了]? You can use it in many situations, like 'You've been studying Chinese for how long?' - [zh:你学中文多长时间了]?

on_screen_text.focus_text: 马克,你来中国多长时间了?
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Mǎkè, nǐ lái Zhōngguó duō cháng shíjiān le?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Mark, how long have you been in China?

highlight_words.explanation_en: A question phrase used to ask about a duration of time.

segments.visual_notes: Highlight '多长时间了' as a set question pattern. Use a calendar or clock icon.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to give a simple, direct answer to a question about duration.

narration.subtitle_en: Mark gives a very concise answer: [zh:六年了]. This is the standard way to respond to a [zh:多长时间了] question. You simply state the duration, in this case [zh:六年] 'six years', and end with the particle [zh:了]. The [zh:了] here indicates that the state of being in China has continued for six years up to the present moment. It's a simple and efficient answer.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 六年了。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Liù nián le.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Six years.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A measure word for years.

segments.visual_notes: Show the number '6' and the character '年' appearing on screen, followed by '了'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to ask someone what kind of work they do in a specific place.

narration.subtitle_en: The conversation starts with a direct question to Mark. It says: [zh:马克,你在中国都做什么工作?] This means 'Mark, what kind of work do you do in China?'. Let's break it down. [zh:你在中国] means 'you in China'. [zh:做] means 'to do'. [zh:什么工作] means 'what job'. The word [zh:都] here implies 'what all' or 'what kinds of' work, suggesting the speaker thinks Mark might do various things. It's a common way to ask about someone's profession.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 马克,你在中国都做什么工作?
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Mǎkè, nǐ zài Zhōngguó dōu zuò shénme gōngzuò?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Mark, what kind of work do you do in China?

highlight_words.explanation_en: Refers to one's occupation or the act of working.

segments.visual_notes: Animate the breakdown of the sentence, highlighting each part as it's explained.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to state one's profession, especially as a freelancer.

narration.subtitle_en: Mark replies with the first part of his answer: [zh:我是个“自由”职业者]. This translates to 'I'm a 'freelancer'.' The word [zh:我] is 'I', [zh:是] is 'am', and [zh:个] is a measure word. The key term here is [zh:自由职业者]. Notice the quotation marks around [zh:自由] in the text. This is a hint that Mark is using the term in a slightly playful or specific way, which we'll explore next.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我是个“自由”职业者,
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Wǒ shì ge “zìyóu” zhíyè zhě,
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I'm a 'freelancer.'

highlight_words.explanation_en: A person who is self-employed and not necessarily committed to a particular employer long-term.

segments.visual_notes: Highlight the term '自由职业者' and the quotation marks around '自由'.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand the meaning and cultural nuance of '自由职业者'.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's look at [zh:自由职业者]. Literally, [zh:自由] means 'freedom', [zh:职业] means 'profession', and [zh:者] is a suffix for 'person'. So, a 'freedom profession person'. As the language note explains, this term can refer to liberal professions like medicine or law. However, Mark is making a joke by emphasizing [zh:自由], 'freedom', to mean he's a freelancer with a flexible, sometimes unpredictable, work life. It's a modern and common term.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 自由 / 职业 / 者
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: zìyóu / zhíyè / zhě
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: freedom / profession / person (suffix)
on_screen_text.notes: Here, Mark uses it humorously to mean 'freelancer'.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Refers to members of the 'liberal professions' but is often used to mean 'freelancer', emphasizing the 'freedom' aspect.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: 自由 (freedom) + 职业 (profession) + 者 (-er, person). A person with a 'free' profession.

segments.visual_notes: Show the breakdown of the word into its components with their meanings.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to list past experiences using the particle '过'.

narration.subtitle_en: Mark continues by listing his jobs: [zh:当过英文家教,演过电视剧,还拍过广告]. This means 'I've been an English tutor, acted in TV dramas, and also filmed commercials.' The particle [zh:过] is used after a verb to indicate a past experience. So, [zh:当过] means 'have been', [zh:演过] means 'have acted', and [zh:拍过] means 'have filmed'. The word [zh:还] means 'also' or 'in addition'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 当过英文家教,演过电视剧,还拍过广告,
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: dāng guo yīngwén jiājiào, yǎn guo diànshìjù, hái pāi guo guǎnggào,
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I've been an English tutor, acted in TV dramas, and filmed commercials.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Verb '当' (to serve as) + particle '过' (past experience).
Verb '演' (to act) + particle '过' (past experience).
Verb '拍' (to shoot/film) + particle '过' (past experience).

segments.visual_notes: Highlight the '过' particle in each verb phrase as it's explained.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to add more information using '有时候' and '搞点儿'.

narration.subtitle_en: Mark finishes his list with: [zh:有时候也搞点儿翻译]. This means 'Sometimes I also do some translation.' [zh:有时候] means 'sometimes'. [zh:也] means 'also'. The phrase [zh:搞点儿] is a colloquial way of saying 'to do a bit of something'. Here, [zh:搞点儿翻译] means 'to do a bit of translation'. It sounds more casual than saying [zh:做翻译].

on_screen_text.focus_text: 有时候也搞点儿翻译。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: yǒushíhou yě gǎo diǎnr fānyì.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Sometimes I also do some translation.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Literally 'have time', it's a common adverb for 'sometimes'.
A versatile and informal verb often used to mean 'to do' or 'to work on'.
Can be a noun (translation) or a verb (to translate).

segments.visual_notes: Emphasize the colloquial nature of '搞点儿'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to express that someone looks familiar.

narration.subtitle_en: Upon hearing this, the speaker has a moment of realization: [zh:我说怎么有点儿面熟呢]. This phrase means 'I was wondering why you look a bit familiar.' The opening [zh:我说] doesn't mean 'I say'; here, it's an interjection like 'No wonder!' or 'So that's why!'. [zh:怎么] means 'how' or 'why'. [zh:有点儿面熟] means 'a little bit familiar-looking'. The particle [zh:呢] at the end softens the tone.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我说怎么有点儿面熟呢,
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Wǒ shuō zěnme yǒudiǎnr miànshú ne,
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I was wondering why you look a bit familiar.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A combination of '面' (face) and '熟' (familiar).
highlight_words.character_insight_en: 面 (miàn) means 'face' and 熟 (shú) means 'familiar' or 'cooked'. A 'familiar face'.

segments.visual_notes: A lightbulb icon could appear next to '我说' to signify realization.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to state that you have seen something someone was in.

narration.subtitle_en: The speaker confirms their memory: [zh:我看过你演的电视剧]. This means 'I've seen the TV drama you were in.' Again, we see the particle [zh:过] indicating a past experience: [zh:看过] means 'have seen'. The phrase [zh:你演的] means 'that you acted in', and it modifies [zh:电视剧], 'TV drama'. This is a very common sentence pattern to connect a person with their work.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我看过你演的电视剧。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: wǒ kàn guo nǐ yǎn de diànshìjù.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I've seen the TV drama you were in.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Literally 'electric vision play'.

segments.visual_notes: Draw a line connecting '你演的' to '电视剧' to show the modification.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to compliment someone's performance using the 'V + 得 + Adv' structure.

narration.subtitle_en: Finally, a compliment: [zh:你演得很不错]. This means 'You acted very well.' This sentence uses a very important structure for compliments: Verb + [zh:得] + Adverb. Here, the verb is [zh:演] (to act), and the adverb describing the action is [zh:很不错] (very good). [zh:不错] literally means 'not wrong', but it's a common way to say 'good' or 'pretty good'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 你演得很不错。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Nǐ yǎn de hěn búcuò.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: You acted very well.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A common and positive way to say something is good. Literally 'not wrong'.

segments.visual_notes: Highlight the '演', '得', and '很不错' parts to show the grammatical structure.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn a humble way to respond to a compliment.

narration.subtitle_en: Mark responds humbly: [zh:不过是三流演员而已]. This means 'I'm just a third-rate actor.' This is a self-deprecating comment, which is common in Chinese culture when receiving a compliment. The structure [zh:不过是...而已] means 'it's just...' or 'nothing more than...'. [zh:三流] literally means 'third-stream' or 'third-rate', implying not very good. [zh:演员] means 'actor'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 不过是三流演员而已。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Búguò shì sānliú yǎnyuán éryǐ.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I'm just a third-rate actor.
on_screen_text.notes: A common structure for downplaying something: 不过是...而已 (búguò shì...éryǐ).

highlight_words.explanation_en: A term used to describe something or someone of low quality. '三' is three, '流' is stream or class.
A particle used at the end of a sentence to express 'only' or 'just'.

segments.visual_notes: Show the structure '不过是...而已' bracketing the main phrase.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to make an observation using '看起来'.

narration.subtitle_en: The speaker makes an observation based on Mark's list of jobs: [zh:看起来你的工作机会很多,很忙啊]. This means 'It seems like you have a lot of job opportunities and are very busy.' The phrase [zh:看起来] means 'it looks like' or 'it seems'. [zh:工作机会] is 'job opportunities'. [zh:很多] means 'many', and [zh:很忙] means 'very busy'. The final particle [zh:啊] adds a tone of exclamation or realization.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 看起来你的工作机会很多,很忙啊。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Kànqǐlái nǐ de gōngzuò jīhuì hěn duō, hěn máng a.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: It seems like you have a lot of job opportunities and are very busy.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A common phrase to express an opinion based on observation.
Refers to a chance or opportunity to do something.

segments.visual_notes: Highlight '看起来' at the beginning of the sentence.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to express 'not necessarily' and describe a fluctuating situation.

narration.subtitle_en: Mark clarifies his situation: [zh:不一定,有的时候很忙,有的时候没事做]. This means 'Not necessarily. Sometimes I'm busy, other times I have nothing to do.' [zh:不一定] is a great phrase for 'not necessarily' or 'it depends'. He then uses a parallel structure, [zh:有的时候...有的时候...], which means 'sometimes... other times...'. [zh:没事做] literally means 'have no thing to do'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 不一定,有的时候很忙,有的时候没事做。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Bú yídìng, yǒude shíhou hěn máng, yǒude shíhou méi shì zuò.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Not necessarily. Sometimes I'm busy, other times I have nothing to do.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Used to express uncertainty or that something is not fixed.

segments.visual_notes: Visually align the two '有的时候' phrases to emphasize the parallel structure.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to say 'to be frank' and describe work as 'unstable'.

narration.subtitle_en: Mark continues with a frank admission: [zh:说白了,实际上是工作有点儿不稳定]. This means 'To be frank, the work is a bit unstable.' The phrase [zh:说白了] is a key expression we'll look at more closely. [zh:实际上] means 'actually' or 'in reality'. [zh:工作有点儿不稳定] means 'the work is a bit unstable'. [zh:不稳定] is the opposite of stable.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 说白了,实际上是工作有点儿不稳定。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Shuō bái le, shíjìshang shì gōngzuò yǒudiǎnr bù wěndìng.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: To be frank, the work is a bit unstable.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A phrase used to introduce a blunt or straightforward statement.
The prefix '不' (bù) negates '稳定' (wěndìng), which means stable.

segments.visual_notes: Highlight '说白了' as an introductory phrase.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand how and when to use the phrase '说白了'.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's focus on the phrase [zh:说白了]. It literally means 'spoken plainly'. You use it when you're about to get to the heart of the matter, or say something directly and honestly, perhaps something that was previously implied. The character [zh:白] here means 'plain' or 'unadorned'. So you are 'speaking plainly'. It's a great way to signal that you're cutting through the fluff and giving the straight truth.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 说白了
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: shuō bái le
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: to speak frankly; to put it bluntly
on_screen_text.notes: Use this phrase to introduce a direct and honest explanation.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Means 'to speak frankly' or 'to put it bluntly'. The character '白' (bái) here means 'plain' or 'unadorned'.

segments.visual_notes: Show the characters '说' (speak) and '白' (plain) with their meanings to build up the phrase's meaning.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn common phrases for reassurance like 'it's okay' and 'no problem'.

narration.subtitle_en: Despite the instability, Mark is not worried. He says: [zh:不过没关系,生活没问题]. This means 'But it's okay, life is not a problem.' [zh:不过] is a common transition word for 'however' or 'but'. [zh:没关系] is a very useful phrase meaning 'it doesn't matter' or 'it's okay'. And [zh:生活没问题] means 'life is no problem'. These are great phrases to express a positive or relaxed attitude towards a challenge.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 不过没关系,生活没问题。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Búguò méi guānxi, shēnghuó méi wèntí.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: But it's okay, life is not a problem.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A conjunction used to introduce a contrasting idea.
A very common phrase for reassurance or accepting an apology.
Literally 'no problem', used to express capability or agreement.

segments.visual_notes: Show '没关系' and '没问题' side-by-side to compare these useful phrases.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to describe one's state of life and mention making friends.

narration.subtitle_en: Mark concludes on a happy note: [zh:在这儿,我生活得很快乐,交了很多中国朋友]. This means 'Here, I live very happily and have made many Chinese friends.' [zh:在这儿] means 'here'. The structure [zh:生活得很快乐] is another example of Verb + [zh:得] + Adverb, meaning 'to live happily'. Finally, [zh:交了很多中国朋友] means 'made many Chinese friends'. The verb for making friends is [zh:交].

on_screen_text.focus_text: 在这儿,我生活得很快乐,交了很多中国朋友。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Zài zhèr, wǒ shēnghuó de hěn kuàilè, jiāo le hěn duō Zhōngguó péngyou.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Here, I live very happily and have made many Chinese friends.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Can be a noun for 'life' or a verb for 'to live'.
The standard verb phrase for making friends.

segments.visual_notes: Highlight the 'V + 得 + Adv' structure in '生活得很快乐'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to use '难怪' to express that something now makes sense.

narration.subtitle_en: Hearing that Mark has many Chinese friends, the speaker connects the dots. They say: [zh:难怪你的中文这么好]. This means 'No wonder your Chinese is so good.' The word [zh:难怪] is the key here. It means 'no wonder' and is used when you've just learned a piece of information that explains something you were curious about. [zh:这么好] means 'so good'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 难怪你的中文这么好。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Nánguài nǐ de Zhōngwén zhème hǎo.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: No wonder your Chinese is so good.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Used at the beginning of a sentence to express that something is now understood or not surprising.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: 难 (nán) means 'difficult' and 怪 (guài) means 'to blame' or 'strange'. So, it's 'not difficult to find it strange', or simply 'no wonder'.

segments.visual_notes: A puzzle piece clicking into place could be a good visual for '难怪'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to praise someone as 'remarkable' using '不简单'.

narration.subtitle_en: The conversation shifts to Li Zhe's brother's friend. The speaker says: [zh:李哲,你哥哥的朋友真不简单啊]. This means 'Li Zhe, your brother's friend is really something.' The phrase [zh:真不简单] is a high compliment. [zh:简单] means 'simple', so [zh:不简单] means 'not simple' or 'remarkable'. [zh:真] means 'really', and [zh:啊] is an exclamation particle. It's a great way to say someone is impressive.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 李哲,你哥哥的朋友真不简单啊,
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Lǐ Zhé, nǐ gēge de péngyou zhēn bù jiǎndān a,
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Li Zhe, your brother's friend is really something/remarkable,

highlight_words.explanation_en: A common way to praise someone as being impressive, capable, or complex.

segments.visual_notes: Show '简单' (simple) being crossed out and replaced with '不简单' (remarkable).

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to express surprise that something happened at a young age.

narration.subtitle_en: The speaker explains why he's so remarkable: [zh:这么年轻就当上总经理了]. This means 'He became a general manager at such a young age.' The structure [zh:这么] + Adjective + [zh:就] + Verb phrase is used to express surprise that something happened so early or easily. [zh:这么年轻] means 'so young'. [zh:当上] means 'to become (a manager)', and [zh:总经理] is 'general manager'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 这么年轻就当上总经理了。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: zhème niánqīng jiù dāngshang zǒngjīnglǐ le.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: He became a general manager at such a young age.
on_screen_text.notes: Structure: 这么 (zhème) + Adj. + 就 (jiù) + Verb

highlight_words.explanation_en: Literally 'year-light', meaning young in age.
'总' (general/chief) + '经理' (manager).

segments.visual_notes: Highlight the '这么...就...' structure to show the cause of surprise.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the '不但...也...' structure to list multiple qualities.

narration.subtitle_en: Li Zhe adds more details about his friend: [zh:他不但懂销售,也是个很好的管理人材]. This means 'Not only does he know sales, he is also a very good manager.' This sentence uses the [zh:不但...也...] structure, which means 'not only... but also...'. [zh:懂销售] means 'to understand sales'. [zh:管理人材] means 'management talent'. This is a very useful pattern for listing two related, positive attributes.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 他不但懂销售,也是个很好的管理人材。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Tā búdàn dǒng xiāoshòu, yě shì ge hěn hǎo de guǎnlǐ réncái.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Not only does he know sales, he is also a very good manager.
on_screen_text.notes: Structure: 不但...也... (búdàn...yě...) means 'not only...but also...'

highlight_words.explanation_en: A common conjunction pair for connecting two clauses.
Refers to the act of selling or the sales department.
A person of ability or talent. Also written as 人才.

segments.visual_notes: Animate the '不但' and '也' to show they are a pair.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to express continuous improvement with '越...越...'.

narration.subtitle_en: He then describes the result of his friend's leadership: [zh:听说在他的领导下,公司越办越好]. This means 'I hear that under his leadership, the company is doing better and better.' [zh:听说] means 'I heard'. [zh:在他的领导下] means 'under his leadership'. The key structure is [zh:越办越好], which uses the '越 A 越 B' pattern to mean 'the more A, the more B'. Here, it means the company is run 'more and more' well.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 听说在他的领导下,公司越办越好。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Tīngshuō zài tā de lǐngdǎo xià, gōngsī yuè bàn yuè hǎo.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I hear that under his leadership, the company is doing better and better.
on_screen_text.notes: Structure: 越...越... (yuè...yuè...) means 'more and more'.

highlight_words.translation: to be told, I hear that
highlight_words.explanation_en: Used to report something one has heard from others.
Can be a noun for 'leader' or 'leadership'.
A structure indicating that the degree of B changes with the degree of A.

segments.visual_notes: An upward-trending arrow could visualize the meaning of '越办越好'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to use '越来越' to describe a trend.

narration.subtitle_en: The conversation broadens to a general trend. The speaker observes: [zh:现在在中国的外国人越来越多]. This means 'There are more and more foreigners in China now.' [zh:现在] is 'now'. The structure [zh:越来越] is similar to '越...越...' but is used with an adjective to mean 'more and more'. So, [zh:越来越多] means 'more and more numerous'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 现在在中国的外国人越来越多,
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Xiànzài zài Zhōngguó de wàiguórén yuèláiyuè duō,
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: There are more and more foreigners in China now,
on_screen_text.notes: Structure: 越来越 (yuèláiyuè) + Adj. means 'more and more...'

highlight_words.explanation_en: Literally 'outside-country-person'.
Used before an adjective to show a continuing trend.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to ask if someone is accustomed to something.

narration.subtitle_en: The speaker finishes with a question to the foreigners in the group: [zh:你们习惯中国的生活吗?]. This means 'Are you used to life in China?' [zh:你们] is the plural 'you'. The key verb is [zh:习惯], which means 'to be accustomed to' or 'to be used to'. [zh:中国的生活] means 'life in China'. The particle [zh:吗] at the end turns the statement into a yes-no question.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 你们习惯中国的生活吗?
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: nǐmen xíguàn Zhōngguó de shēnghuó ma?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Are you used to life in China?

highlight_words.explanation_en: Can be a verb meaning 'to be accustomed to' or a noun meaning 'habit'.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand a rhetorical question and how to describe daily activities.

narration.subtitle_en: The speaker starts with [zh:你说呢], a rhetorical question that means 'What do you think?' or 'Isn't it obvious?'. It implies the answer is clear. He then describes his happy life: [zh:我在你们家] means 'at your house'. [zh:每天] is 'every day'. The main activity is [zh:跟你父母打太极拳], 'practice Tai Chi with your parents'. This part of the sentence sets a very positive and relaxed tone.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 你说呢？我在你们家，每天跟你父母打太极拳，
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Nǐ shuō ne? Wǒ zài nǐmen jiā, měitiān gēn nǐ fùmǔ dǎ tàijíquán,
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: What do you think? At your house, I practice tai chi with your parents every day,

highlight_words.explanation_en: A form of Chinese martial art practiced for both its defense training and its health benefits.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the '又...又...' structure and how to express strong feelings with '都'.

narration.subtitle_en: Continuing his thought, he says he eats food that is [zh:又好吃、又健康]. The structure [zh:又] A [zh:又] B means 'both A and B'. Here, the food is both 'delicious' and 'healthy'. The sentence ends with [zh:都不想回去了], which strongly expresses his feeling. [zh:都] here adds emphasis, so it means 'I don't even want to go back.' This shows how content he is.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 吃他们做的又好吃、又健康的饭菜，都不想回去了。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: chī tāmen zuò de yòu hǎochī, yòu jiànkāng de fàncài, dōu bùxiǎng huíqù le.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: eat the delicious and healthy food they make. I don't even want to go back.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A structure used to connect two adjectives or verbs that describe the same subject.
Describes a state of being well in body and mind.
A general term for food or dishes served in a meal.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand how to use '更' to mean 'even more' for emphasis.

narration.subtitle_en: This is a short but powerful response. The key word here is [zh:更], which means 'even more' or 'to a greater degree'. So, when the speaker says [zh:我更没问题了], she means 'I have even less of a problem' or 'For me, it's absolutely not a problem.' It shows a higher level of confidence and ease compared to the previous speakers.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我更没问题了。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Wǒ gèng méi wèntí le.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I have even less of a problem.

highlight_words.explanation_en: An adverb used to indicate a greater degree or extent.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to ask about an ongoing activity using '还'.

narration.subtitle_en: Here we have a simple question. The word [zh:还] means 'still' or 'in addition'. In this context, [zh:你还写博客吗] asks, 'Do you still write your blog?'. It implies the speaker knows the person used to write a blog and is checking if the activity is continuing. [zh:博客] is the word for 'blog'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 你还写博客吗？
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Nǐ hái xiě bókè ma?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Do you still write your blog?

segments.teaching_goal: To understand how to express certainty and the idea of a never-ending supply.

narration.subtitle_en: The response begins with [zh:当然], 'of course,' showing strong affirmation. The speaker explains why: here, every day she sees ([zh:看到]) and hears ([zh:听到]) [zh:新鲜的事], which means 'new things'. The final phrase, [zh:永远也写不完], is very expressive. [zh:永远] means 'forever,' and [zh:写不完] is a potential complement meaning 'can't finish writing.' Together, it means 'I'll never run out of things to write.'

highlight_words.explanation_en: Used to express certainty or agreement.
Can describe food, air, or in this case, experiences and things that are new and interesting.
An adverb indicating that something lasts for all time.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand idiomatic expressions and the concept of '融入社会'.

narration.subtitle_en: This sentence has a few interesting parts. It starts with [zh:我就不用说了], an idiom meaning 'It goes without saying for me' or 'I don't even need to say it.' Then comes a rhetorical question: [zh:要不然怎么会住这么久呢] which means 'otherwise, how could I have lived here for so long?'. The main point is in the last clause: [zh:我觉得自己已经融入这个社会了], 'I feel I've already integrated into this society.' [zh:融入] means 'to merge into' or 'to integrate.'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我就不用说了，要不然怎么会住这么久呢？我觉得自己已经融入这个社会了。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Wǒ jiù búyòng shuō le, yàoburán zěnme huì zhù zhème jiǔ ne? Wǒ juéde zìjǐ yǐjīng róngrù zhège shèhuì le.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I don't even need to say it. Otherwise, how could I have lived here for so long? I feel I've already integrated into this society.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A conjunction used to introduce a consequence if the preceding condition is not met.
To merge with or become a part of a group or society.
Refers to the community of people living in a particular country or region.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to express past worries and current relief.

narration.subtitle_en: The speaker expresses a change of heart. First, she describes a past worry: [zh:我原来还怕自己适应不了这儿的生活]. [zh:原来] means 'originally,' and [zh:适应不了] means 'unable to adapt.' Then, she explains what caused the change: [zh:听了你们的话], 'after hearing what you all said.' The result is [zh:我放心多了], 'I'm much more relieved.' [zh:放心] is a key verb meaning 'to set one's mind at ease.'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我原来还怕自己适应不了这儿的生活。听了你们的话，我放心多了。

on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I was originally afraid I wouldn't be able to adapt to life here. After hearing what you all said, I'm much more relieved.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand phrases related to international mobility and development.

[zh:发展] is a key word for 'development' or 'to develop'. The second part is [zh:中国人到国外学习、工作], 'Chinese people go abroad to study and work.' [zh:国外] means 'overseas' or 'abroad'. This sets the stage for the conclusion about the world getting smaller.

on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Now foreigners come to China to develop their careers, and Chinese people go abroad to study and work.

Then, [zh:关系近了] 'relationships have gotten closer.' This all leads to the final, thematic statement: [zh:世界变小了], 'the world has gotten smaller.' This is a metaphor for how technology and travel have made us all feel more connected.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 大家的联系多了，关系近了，世界变小了。

A verb indicating a transformation from one state to another.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the common phrases for proposing a toast.

narration.subtitle_en: The speaker begins by complimenting Li Wen: [zh:说得真好], 'well said.' Then, he initiates a toast. He gets everyone's attention with [zh:朋友们，来], 'Friends, come on.' [zh:大家举起杯来] means 'everyone raise your glasses.' The most important part is the toast itself: [zh:为我们的友谊干杯]. The structure [zh:为]...[zh:干杯] means 'a toast to...'. Here, it's a toast to 'our friendship', [zh:友谊].

on_screen_text.focus_text: 李文说得真好。朋友们，来，大家举起杯来，为我们的友谊干杯！

The state of being friends.
Literally 'dry the cup,' it's the standard expression for 'cheers!' when drinking.

[zh:事业] means 'career' or 'enterprise,' and [zh:成功] means 'success.' This shows how versatile the toasting pattern is for celebrating different achievements or wishing people well.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn and use the common farewell expression '一路平安'.

narration.subtitle_en: This is a common and warm-hearted farewell. The sentence starts with [zh:祝], which means 'to wish.' The key phrase is [zh:一路平安]. [zh:一路] means 'the whole way' or 'along the journey,' and [zh:平安] means 'safe and sound.' So, [zh:一路平安] is a classic way to say 'Have a safe trip!' or 'Bon voyage!' to someone who is about to travel.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 祝天明和丽莎一路平安！
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Zhù Tiānmíng hé Lìshā yílù píng'ān!

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to express a common well-wish for health, often used in toasts or greetings.

narration.subtitle_en: This sentence, [zh:祝大家身体健康], is a very common and warm-hearted toast or blessing in Chinese culture. Let's break it down. [zh:祝] means 'to wish'. [zh:大家] means 'everyone'. [zh:身体] means 'body' or 'health', and [zh:健康] means 'healthy'. So, literally, it's 'Wish everyone body healthy.' You'll often hear this during meals, especially at celebrations, festivals, or when toasting with elders. It's a sincere way to show you care about others' well-being.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 祝大家身体健康!
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Zhù dàjiā shēntǐ jiànkāng!
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Wishing everyone good health!

highlight_words.explanation_en: A verb used to express good wishes.
A pronoun referring to everybody present.
Refers to one's physical body and, by extension, their health.
An adjective meaning 'in good health'.

segments.visual_notes: Animate icons for 'wish' (like a shooting star), 'everyone' (group of people), and 'health' (a heart or a plus sign).

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the Chinese equivalent of 'Cheers!' and understand its cultural usage.

narration.subtitle_en: Next, we have [zh:干杯], which is the Chinese way to say 'Cheers!'. Let's look at the characters. [zh:干] means 'dry' and [zh:杯] means 'cup' or 'glass'. So, [zh:干杯] literally means 'to dry the cup,' implying you should drink everything in your glass. While it literally means to empty your glass, in many casual situations today, it's acceptable to just take a sip. However, in more formal banquets, people might still drink the whole glass, so it's good to observe what others are doing.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 干杯!
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Gānbēi!
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Cheers!

highlight_words.explanation_en: A common toast, literally meaning 'dry the cup'.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: Composed of 干 (gān, dry) and 杯 (bēi, cup). The combination directly translates to the action of emptying one's cup in a toast.

segments.visual_notes: Show an animation of two glasses clinking, with the liquid inside disappearing to illustrate 'dry the cup'.

segments.teaching_goal: To review and summarize the principles of Chinese word order, including basic structure, topic-comment sentences, and chronological/general-to-specific ordering.

narration.subtitle_en: Now, let's dive into grammar. We'll start with a review of Chinese word order. The basic structure is Subject, followed by an adverbial, then the verb, and finally the object. For example, in the sentence [zh:柯林去超市买了很多饮料], 'Ke Lin' is the subject, [zh:去超市] 'goes to the supermarket' is an adverbial phrase of location and purpose, [zh:买] 'buys' is the verb, and [zh:很多饮料] 'many drinks' is the object. Another key principle is that events are stated in chronological order. In [zh:我在房间用电脑写文章], you are first in the room, then you use the computer, and then you write. The order of phrases reflects the order of actions. Similarly, time and location are described from general to specific. For instance, dates go from year to month to day, the opposite of English. Finally, remember the topic-comment structure, where known information, the topic, comes first. For example, in [zh:公司越办越好], the company is the topic, and its improving state is the comment.

on_screen_text.focus_text: Subject — (Adverbial) — Verb — (Object)
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Basic Chinese Sentence Structure
on_screen_text.notes: Events are typically ordered chronologically and from general to specific.

grammar_points.pattern: 柯林去超市买了很多饮料。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Kē Lín qù chāoshì mǎi le hěn duō yǐnliào.
grammar_points.explanation_en: Basic S-V-O structure. The adverbial phrase [去超市] indicates where the action takes place.

grammar_points.pattern: 公司越办越好。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Gōngsī yuè bàn yuè hǎo.
grammar_points.explanation_en: Topic-Comment structure. '公司' (the company) is the topic, and '越办越好' (is doing better and better) is the comment about it.

grammar_points.pattern: 2020年5月27号上午9点30分
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: èr líng èr líng nián wǔ yuè èrshíqī hào shàngwǔ jiǔ diǎn sānshí fēn
grammar_points.explanation_en: General to Specific: Time is ordered from the largest unit (year) to the smallest (minute).

grammar_points.pattern: 我在房间用电脑写文章。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Wǒ zài fángjiān yòng diànnǎo xiě wénzhāng.
grammar_points.explanation_en: Chronological Order: The sequence of phrases (being in the room -> using the computer -> writing) follows the natural order of events.

segments.teaching_goal: To review the usage of the 把 construction, focusing on when it's required and its specific structural rules.

narration.subtitle_en: Next, let's review the [zh:把] construction. This structure is used to emphasize what happens to a specific, known object. It's required in a few key situations. First, when there's an indirect object, like in [zh:你把这张照片送给我吧], 'give this photo to me.' Second, when you specify the location of an object after an action, for example, [zh:他把筷子放在了桌子上], 'he put the chopsticks on the table.' Third, when the verb is followed by the complement [zh:成], meaning 'to become'. A crucial rule is that negative adverbs like [zh:没] or [zh:不] must come *before* [zh:把]. In our dialogue, Li Zhe asks, [zh:他没把这个消息告诉你], 'He didn't tell you this news?'. Notice [zh:没] is placed before [zh:把]. Also, remember that potential complements cannot be used in a [zh:把] sentence.

on_screen_text.focus_text: Subject + 把 + Object + Verb + Other Elements
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Subject + bǎ + Object + Verb + Other Elements
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: This structure emphasizes the result or influence of an action on an object.

grammar_points.pattern: Q: 他没把这个消息告诉你？
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Tā méi bǎ zhège xiāoxi gàosu nǐ?
grammar_points.explanation_en: Rule: Negative adverbs like [没] must be placed before [把].

grammar_points.pattern: 你把这张照片送给我吧。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Nǐ bǎ zhè zhāng zhàopiàn sòng gěi wǒ ba.
grammar_points.explanation_en: Use [把] when a verb takes both a direct and an indirect object.

grammar_points.pattern: 他把宿舍房间变成了一个垃圾场。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Tā bǎ sùshè fángjiān biàn chéng le yí ge lājīchǎng.
grammar_points.explanation_en: Use [把] when the verb is followed by the resultative complement [成].

grammar_points.pattern: Incorrect: 我把购房合同找不到了。
grammar_points.explanation_en: Rule: Potential complements (e.g., 找不到) cannot be used in [把] sentences.

segments.visual_notes: Highlight the position of '没' before '把' in the example sentence. Use icons to represent the 'indirect object', 'location', and 'transformation' (成) scenarios.

segments.teaching_goal: To review the key vocabulary and grammar patterns from the lesson.

narration.subtitle_en: Great job! Let's quickly review the highlights of this lesson. We learned about celebrating, welcoming friends, and adapting to life in a new country. Try to remember these key phrases and structures as they are very useful.

on_screen_text.focus_text: Key Phrases Review

highlight_words.translation: to host a welcome / farewell meal for...

highlight_words.word: 互相帮助, 互相照顾
highlight_words.translation: help and look after each other
to be frank, to put it bluntly

segments.visual_notes: Display the key phrases as a list on a final summary card. Each phrase can appear with a simple, relevant icon.
